data.title: 4 'Safer' Dividend Buys In Barron's 23 Better March Bets Than T-Bills
data.description:
Foreword

While most of this collection of Barron's Better Bets (BBB) is pricey or reveals disappointing dividends, four of the ten highest-yield Dogs with the "Safest" dividends of the BBB are ready to buy. March finds Verizon (VZ), Keycorp (KEY), Regions Financial (RF), and Kinder Morgan (KMI) as the buyable top four, living up to the dogcatcher ideal. That is, each showed an annual dividend from $1K invested exceeding its single share price.

The remaining six of the March "Safer" twelve have prices exceeding the dividend returned from $1k invested. Of the six, Truist Financial (TFC) is closest to the mark with a price within $0.05. Furthermore, Altria Group (MO) is within $1.23 of parity.

With renewed downside market pressure of 54.04%, it will be possible for all ten of the top BBB "Safer" dividend dogs to become elite fair-priced, with their annual yield (from $1K invested) meeting or exceeding their single share prices.

[See a summary of the top ten fair-priced March BBB Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and the aforementioned ten as "safer" choices in the Afterword, at the bottom of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 17.06% To 28.42% Net Gains From Top-Ten BBB Dogs By March 2027

Four of the ten top-yield BBB Dogs (shaded in the chart below) were among the top-ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this March, the 2026 yield-based forecast for BBB dogs, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 40% accurate.

Estimated dividend returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the projections below. Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to March 2027 were:

Truist Financial was projected to net $284.19 based on the median of target price estimates from 22 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 12% under the market as a whole.

KeyCorp was projected to net $283.64, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 22 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 8% more than the market as a whole.

U.S. Bancorp (USB) was projected to net $259.00, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 5% greater than the market as a whole.

Best Buy Co. (BBY) was projected to net $236.87, based on the median of target price estimates from 26 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 46% greater than the market as a whole.

Regions Financial was projected to net $209.38, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 3% over the market as a whole.

Equity Residential (EQR) was projected to net $201.27 based on the median of target prices estimated by 25 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 25% less than the market as a whole.

Philip Morris International (PM) was projected to net $192.83, based on the median of target price estimates from 17 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 62% less than the market as a whole.

Conagra Brands (CAG) was projected to net $179.63, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 17 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 2% opposite the market as a whole.

UDR Inc. (UDR) was projected to net $174.69, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 22 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 29% under the market as a whole.

Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA) was projected to net $170.58, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 25 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 21% under the market as a whole.

The average net gain in price was estimated at 21.92% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten BBB stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 19% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks become dividend dogs by showing three key qualities: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, and (2) their prices fall. Thus, (3) yield (dividend/price) grows higher than their peers. Thus, the highest-yielding stocks in any collection become valued as "dividend dogs." More specifically, they may be, in fact, best named "underdogs," owing to their contrarian nature.

February BBB Total Returns

February BBB Broker Targets

The February, 2026 BBB 23 By Yield

Actionable Conclusion: March Top Ten BBB Stocks By Yield Ranged 4.64% To 8.25% Per YCharts

The top ten March BBB Dogs occupied six of eleven Morningstar sectors.

In first place, was the first of two consumer staple stalwarts, Conagra Brands [1]. The other placed second, Altria Group [2].

After those, a lone healthcare sector member took second place, Pfizer (PFE) [2]. Then, a lone consumer discretionary stock placed fourth, Best Buy Co [4].

The communication services sector member placed fifth, Verizon [5], followed by the first of four real estate sector dogs in the sixth and eighth, to tenth positions: Realty Income (O) [6], Mid-America Apartment Communities [8], UDR Inc. (UDR) [9], and Simon Property Group (SPG) [10]

Finally, a lone energy representative, in seventh place, ONEOK (OKE) [7] completed the March Dogs of the BBB by yield.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The top graph above shows the relative strengths of the top ten March BBB dogs by dividend and price as of market close 3/11/2026.

BBB Prices per Dividend Dollar

The full chart above reveals twenty-three BBB dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock. The least expensive dog of the BBB is Conagra Brands at $12.13 per dividend dollar, and the most expensive is Entergy (ETR) at $40.55 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Four of Twenty-Three BBB Dogs Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Four BBB companies so infected are LyondellBasell, Federal Realty, Williams Companies, and Entergy. These are considered unsafe to buy. Furthermore, their infection explains why they are missing from the IDEAL and "safer" dividend dog reckonings.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pullbacks in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pullback opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, tariffs, trade-offs, war games, rants, and uncertainty.

Price Drops, Dividend Increases or Dividend Splits Could Get All Ten BBB Dogs Back to "Fair Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust the share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, four of the top ten are already ideally priced. Three more are in striking distance of Ideal, while three others need double-digit help to cope.

The alternative, of course, could be that these companies raise or split dividends. That may be too much to ask in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile times. Mr. Market is far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts; just watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 10.72% To 25.19% Top Ten BBB Upsides To March, 2026; (32) Three -0.36% to -9.01% Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig out bargains.

Analysts Forecasted A 17.98% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 BBB Dogs As of March 11, 2027

Ten-top BBB dogs were culled by yield for this monthly-update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten BBB dogs selected 3/11/26 by yield, represented six of eleven sectors. (Financial, Industrials, Materials, Technology, and Utilities, missed the top-ten cut.)

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of the Ten Highest-Yield BBB Dogs (34) To Deliver 14.51% Vs. (35) 12.30% Net Gains by All Ten Come March 11, 2027

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten BBB Dividend kennel by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 17.98% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The fifth lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, Best Buy, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 23.69%.

The five lowest-priced BBB top-yield dogs for March were: Conagra; Pfizer; UDR; Verizon; Best Buy, with prices ranging from $16.98 to $63.15.

Five higher-priced Dow top-yield dogs as of March 11 were Realty Income; Altria; ONEOK; Mid-America Apartment, and Simon Property, with prices ranging from $64.71 to $189.56.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added the element of "market sentiment" to note upside potential. Analyst targets provide a present day equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is needed, however, since analysts are historically just 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and only 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As winter blows out and the market warms, the feckless Fed-fueled slide and slithering presidential trade and war pronouncements add momentum into 2026 (with the exception of a few overly speculative and techno-meme stocks). Look for all of the top ten of twenty-three BBB Dogs to become fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or real quick!

Afterword

Buy 4 of 10 'Safer' Dividend March Dogs of the BBBTTB

Mid March 2026, 10 of the 23 BBB were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns with free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. Then four IDEAL of the ten "safer" showed dividend yields from $1000 invested exceeding the price of a single share.

How BBB 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Results Might Produce A "Fair Price" Result For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that four dividends from $1K invested in top ten 'safer' dividend BBB dogs conform to the dogcatcher standard of dividends from $1,000 invested being equal to (or greater than) the price of a single share. Those four ideal dogs were: Verizon, KeyCorp, Regions Financial, and Kinder Morgan.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount, and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock; a so-called "fair price."

A market correction of about $90 could put all ten share prices and a $10 correction would put nine of these ten "safer" survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

There are, of course, other ways for dividends from $1K invested to exceed the price of a single share of stock. That would be for the boards of directors of these companies to raise dividends or make stock splits to the dogcatcher ideal level. That's a lot to ask with all the corporate funds doing so much "good" elsewhere.

BBB IDEAL "Safer" Dividends Revealed Positive Returns with Cash Flow Exceeding Dividend Yields

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher BBB 23 Updates, out here, in public, away from the paywalled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" BBBTTB dog dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article, except as noted, are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

Graphs and charts were compiled by Rydlun & Co., LLC from data derived from Indexarb; YCharts; finance.yahoo.com; analyst mean target price by YCharts. Open source dog art from dividenddogcatcher.com.

data.title: Iran Conflict Triggers A Major Energy Shock
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist | Christian Floro, Market Strategist | Magdalena Ocampo, Market Strategist

Situation update

The coordinated U.S.-Israel strikes on Iran on February 28 and Iran’s subsequent retaliation have pushed the region into its most volatile military confrontation in decades. With fighting still underway, commercial transit through the Strait of Hormuz has effectively ground to a halt, sending oil flows through the chokepoint to near zero.

Damage to energy infrastructure and rapidly filling storage tanks have forced several Gulf producers to curtail output. Most consequentially, QatarEnergy has shut down the Ras Laffan facility, which accounts for roughly 20% of global LNG supply. This represents the single largest disruption to energy supply in modern history.

Unsurprisingly, oil and gas markets have become the focus of investors. Crude prices, already trending higher on geopolitical risk, have climbed from around $51/bbl at the start of 2026 to above $100/bbl, briefly topping $120/bbl early this week.1 Six-month Brent futures have climbed as well, now above $80/bbl, suggesting that investors are beginning to price in a longer conflict.

Although President Trump has signaled optimism that the conflict could be short-lived, the path forward remains highly uncertain. Ultimately, the economic impact will depend on how long shipping lanes through the Strait of Hormuz remain closed and how quickly oil and gas capacity can return after fighting subsides.

Crude oil prices

Brent, daily, dollars per barrel, 2007–present

Market reaction so far

Equities

U.S. equities have shown notable resilience. Nine trading days into the conflict, the S&P 500 is down around 3% and sits less than 5% below its late January peak.2 For context, the index fell 12–13% during the early phases of both the COVID shock and the Liberation Day episode in 2025.

There are two dynamics that likely explain the muted move:

  • Equity markets tend to discount geopolitical shocks given their historically short-lived impact on company earnings.
  • Investors expect that President Trump has limited tolerance for a prolonged energy crisis that could derail his affordability agenda in a midterm election year.

International equities, however, have reacted far more sharply. Asian and European indices are down almost 8% (USD terms), reflecting their far higher sensitivity to energy prices and, in Asia’s case, to Middle East-linked LNG flows.3

Rates and Central Banks

The most pronounced market reaction has been in rates. U.S. Treasury yields have risen as inflation concerns intensify and expectations for 2026 Fed rate cuts this year are pared back.

Moves in Europe have been even more pronounced. Bund yields have risen as markets price in ECB tightening this year, while UK gilt yields have surged as investors rapidly unwind expectations for 2026 rate cuts and begin pricing the risk of hikes.

Market-implied policy rates for December 2026

Movement in expectations between February 27 and March 12

U.S. Dollar

After a period of depreciation, the dollar has reasserted its safe haven role. Its recent outperformance reflects the U.S.’s relatively stronger macro position compared with major net energy importers such as Europe and Japan.

The key tail risk: Prolonged disruption to the Strait of Hormuz

A sustained disruption in the Strait would represent a severe shock to global energy markets:

  • LNG: Roughly 20–25% of global LNG exports pass through Hormuz, primarily from Qatar and the UAE, with about 80% destined for Asia. No meaningful pipeline alternatives exist.
  • Oil: Around 30% of global seaborne oil supply transits the strait, sourced mainly from Saudi Arabia, Iraq, the UAE, Kuwait, Qatar, and Iran. Limited pipeline capacity means re-routing would still leave global supply materially short, while much of the world’s spare capacity would also be inaccessible.

There are three near-term constraints that are amplifying the shock:

  1. Commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively frozen.
  2. Gulf producers have cut output as storage fills; restarting production can take weeks.
  3. Damage to infrastructure (due to Iranian attacks) implies further delays even after hostilities end.

There are mitigants, but they are modest relative to the scale. The G7’s 400-million-barrel release from Strategic Petroleum Reserves provides some relief but cannot fully offset an estimated 20mb daily supply loss. (Note: This is the largest coordinated SPR release on record and more than double the size of the drawdown that followed Russia’s invasion of Ukraine.) Following the announcement, oil prices continued to rise, underscoring both the scale of the disruption and the limited nature of buffers.

U.S. shale could help if prices remain elevated, though producers are unlikely to accelerate drilling unless the conflict looks prolonged. Additionally, while OPEC+ spare capacity can offer some buffer, storage constraints in the Gulf states sharply limit its near‑term effectiveness.

Ultimately, oil prices will depend almost entirely on when transit through the Strait of Hormuz resumes and how quickly productive capacity returns. A return to pre-conflict price levels is far from assured.

A vulnerable moment for the global economy

The global economy appears capable of absorbing a modest, temporary rise in energy prices. U.S. growth remains robust, capital expenditure is strong, and household energy spending as a share of income is near historic lows. Furthermore, corporate profit margins also remain elevated, providing a buffer against higher input costs. Europe is seeing early signs of a recovery in growth, while Asia remains a key engine of global expansion, driven by AI‑related investment, resilient tech exports, and healthy domestic demand. Additionally, energy intensity has declined, reducing the likely economic impact of energy shocks compared with the 1970s.

But vulnerabilities persist. Inflation has eased but remains above central bank targets, and a sustained rise in oil prices would pressure consumers and likely delay rate cuts that had been anticipated at the beginning of the year.

Energy intensity by region

Primary energy consumption per GDP, kilowatt-hours/$, 1975–2022

Implications for U.S. growth and inflation

Historically, oil price shocks have lowered growth and raised inflation. In the U.S., the drag on growth tends to be lagged and partially offset by higher energy investment, while inflation effects are more immediate and can propagate through broader cost channels.

Using a Federal Reserve Board model to quantify the effects of higher crude oil prices:

  • Each $10/bbl increase in oil prices reduces GDP growth by 0.1ppt and raises headline inflation by 0.3ppt after four quarters.
  • At higher price levels, oil price effects become increasingly non-linear, and risks to both growth and inflation widen.

It is also worth noting that, beyond energy, the Middle East plays a critical role in other inputs, including fertilizers (16% of global ammonia, urea, and nitrogen flows) and 25–35% of global helium, a key input in semiconductor manufacturing. As such, the growth impact of a more severe, prolonged conflict would extend beyond the oil price shock.

Given the unusually high uncertainty around both the duration of the conflict and the speed at which energy flows could normalize, it is helpful to anchor expectations around a set of plausible paths. The following scenarios map out how varying degrees of disruption to oil and LNG supply could shape U.S. growth, inflation dynamics, and policy rates, offering a sense of the thresholds at which macro conditions and market behavior could change materially.

Baseline scenario: A sharp but brief oil spike, lasting a few weeks, having negligible growth effects and lifting headline inflation by ~0.2ppt for a quarter, with little impact on core inflation and therefore Fed policy.

Medium adverse scenario: Oil stabilizing around $90/bbl for 2-3 months before only gradually declining would have a more significant impact on growth, yet would still be modest, reducing our full-year U.S. GDP growth forecast of 2.5% only slightly, given that an increase in energy capex would at least partially offset a weakening in consumer spending. On the other hand, core inflation (currently at 2.5%) would rise more significantly to around 3% by end-year, constraining the Fed’s ability to deliver the two rate cuts we currently expect.

Severe adverse scenario (tail risk): A multi‑quarter closure of the Strait of Hormuz could drive oil sustainably above $125/bbl and trigger wider supply chain risks, knocking around 0.5-1% off our 2.5% baseline forecast and pushing core inflation above 3.5% by year-end and potentially force the Fed to consider rate hikes.

Europe: More exposed, less resilient

Europe remains far more vulnerable than the U.S. to prolonged energy pressure. While direct Middle East exposure is below 2022 levels, European gas prices have nearly doubled since January. Growth risks are correspondingly higher.

Yet the regional impacts will likely be uneven. Energy-independent producers such as Norway and Sweden are better insulated, while Italy, Spain, and energy-intensive Germany are more exposed and face significantly greater downside.

Energy imports dependency rate

% of net imports in gross available energy, 2024

Investment implications

Markets are still in the early stages of digesting the conflict, and pricing remains highly dependent on assumptions about how long it will last. So far, earnings expectations remain resilient, but a prolonged energy shock would begin to erode that foundation, negatively impacting risk assets with greater severity.

With signals mixed across asset classes and risks shifting rapidly, a few themes stand out for investors as the energy shock evolves.

  1. Equities and bonds are telling different stories. Equity markets continue to price a short conflict with limited economic fallout, while bond markets are bracing for a longer lasting inflation shock. The eventual path will likely fall somewhere between the two interpretations

  2. The definition of “severe” has shifted higher. Just weeks ago, $100/bbl for oil was considered a tail-risk scenario. Markets must now contend with the possibility that $130/bbl is the new threshold, yet equities have barely reacted, suggesting growing complacency.

  3. Data noise is high. Investors are leaning on unreliable indicators, such as drone attack counts or informal shipping updates, which reinforce uncertainty about the duration of the conflict.

  4. Technology continues to outperform. Alongside energy, tech is the only S&P 500 sector in positive territory this month. Despite rising input cost risks for key Asian semiconductor and memory chip manufacturers, investors see valuation opportunities following February’s tech correction and view the sector’s strong balance sheets as a buffer against macro headwinds.

  5. Country and sector diversification remains critical. Historically, the U.S. outperformed during oil shocks due to its relative energy independence. Still, markets more levered to energy, defense, and inflation resilient sectors, such as Norway, Canada, Brazil, and Saudi Arabia, have often performed even better.

Ultimately, for investors, history argues against dramatic portfolio shifts in response to geopolitical events. In an increasingly fragmented global order, disciplined diversification—across regions, sectors, and assets such as gold, high quality bonds, selective commodities, and defense—remains one of the most effective ways to manage uncertainty while staying aligned with long term objectives.

Footnotes

  1. Crude spiked above $120/bbl on Monday, March 9, during early European trading hours.

  2. March 1, 2026 – March 12, 2026.

  3. March 1, 2026 – March 12, 2026.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Vaalco Energy: Lucky Break
data.description:
VAALCO Energy (EGY) entered fiscal year 2026 expecting to begin the fiscal year with significant negative cash flows. The last article noted that with the FPSO leaving the shipyard and on its way back to the production field, the worst of the expenditures was likely over. The drilling campaign in Gabon had costs first and then producing wells (for cash flow) next. But now production is climbing as the drilling campaign posts some success. The FPSO is expected to be back in the field with production restored by the second half of the year (and possibly as early as the end of May). All of this points to a second half of fiscal year 2026 that is likely to be much better than the start. It also points to a big year for cash flow improvement.

The lucky break comes from the Iranian crisis. This company operates in Western Africa with significant operations in Egypt. The issue with the Iranian crisis is the Strait of Hormuz, where probably close to 20% of the world's oil supply has to travel (through). This company has locations that avoid that situation completely. Therefore, should oil prices spike as expected, the company is expected to rake in the cash at a time when "raking in the cash" is sorely needed to keep borrowings down to a minimum.

Cash Flow

Currently the company has a net borrowing position of very roughly $1 million.

The reason the position is still pretty good is that the company made major progress in collecting Egyptian past due receivables. That process alone resulted in a bonanza in the rough range of $70 million in unexpected (but badly needed) cash income. Egypt always pays. It is just a matter of when.

Until that FPSO is back in place and production is completely restored, there is still likely to be more negative cash flow for the first and second quarters.

This is true even though the Gabon drilling campaign is successfully adding production to improve the cash flow situation.

Earnings

The fourth quarter earnings were anything but normal. The key here is the cash flow shown above. Whenever a company like this has a major project that causes negative cash flow, the major objective is to make sure that the borrowing "does not get out of hand".

That goal far exceeds any earnings report. Clearly, with a net debt position of about $1 million, this company is in an excellent financial position to get the FPSO hooked up and producing. This whole financial situation could have been much worse without the Egyptian past due collections.

There is an impairment charge that is accompanying the sale of the Canadian business. That sale was yet another step by management to keep the debt under control. The unconventional business in Canada was different from really any of the African businesses. Now the Egyptian business may use some of the techniques from that Canadian business (like horizontal wells and "modern completion techniques"). But overall, that sale probably made sense in terms of focusing the company on its profitable core business in Africa.

2026 Capital Budget

In fiscal year 2026, the paramount consideration is the continuation of two large projects begun in fiscal year 2025.

The first major expenditure is the FPSO reconnection to begin production again. The major consideration of possible cost overruns at the shipyard is now a past issue. The market knows the results of repairs and upgrades. The key here is that the company has emerged from this with a strong financial position so far.

The next big step of getting the FPSO back to the field and reconnecting the wells for production startup is considered relatively routine. But it is still a lot of money for a company of this size. Hence there will be some market fears until production restarts. When it comes to a major cash drain (like this project), nothing succeeds with Mr. Market like the end of the project and the start of cash flow.

The next big project underway is the Gabon drilling campaign. But this campaign already has some success. It is therefore already contributing to cash flow even though drilling will continue for several more months. This drilling campaign is likely to be the single most significant contributor to cash flow increases until FPSO production is restored.

Once the FPSO is back online for production, the cash flow of the company is likely to turn positive. Then debt repayment will become a priority. This company has a long history of no debt on the balance sheet. Even though the net debt position is conservative, the borrowing so far represents a significant break with past history. Investors should therefore expect a very high priority of debt repayment and a return to a large cash balance before there is any increase in shareholder returns.

Summary

The untold story here is the success in bringing Egyptian payments current at a time when that cash was sorely needed. To say that it prevented a lot of unnecessary borrowing (and hence interest expense) is an understatement.

Even if Egypt backslides, as it is prone to do from time to time, the company is at the point where positive cash flow is just on the horizon. Positive cash flow could allow one of those periodic backslides without a material change in financial position.

This is a strong buy idea because management has a fairly long-stated goal of tripling production by 2030. Production increases from the lows of this strategy actually began in fiscal year 2025. This fiscal year, production should increase throughout the fiscal year.

The restart of production in Côte d'Ivoire should mark the start of positive cash flow not long afterwards. That will mark the end of large cash expenditures to repair and upgrade the FPSO (and, of course, get it back to the field and hookups for regular production). The swing between cash outflows for the expense of that project and cash inflows from regular production should be substantial.

But that makes VAALCO Energy a strong buy idea. In this frothy market, the company is about to post some strong production comparisons, aided by an Iranian crisis that may well push commodity prices skywards for a bit. For at least the next four years, this is a growth story that is fairly rare in this industry.

I would also consider holding this idea until management sells the company or the current story changes materially.

Risks

There could be a quick end to the Iranian situation. That may well dampen the expected benefits of higher oil prices. Right now, that seems unlikely. But investors may want to use the current situation to raise cash for however long it lasts and then worry about things like strong buys later.

Even if the current situation lasts a year, that is still not all that much time to get ready for what likely follows. Right now, there is a lot of talk about repeats of scary scenarios like the 1970s and even 2008. Whether it happens is another matter entirely.

Along with that scary talk is the other side, which claims both situations could have been avoided. We will soon see if that is the case here.

What really needs to happen is the political considerations need to fade from dominance. Until that happens, fundamentals may not matter as much as politics for this industry. That could make future analysis a real challenge.

data.title: Arbor Series F Preferreds: The Overlooked Reset Trade
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Arbor Series F Preferreds (ABR.PR.F) might seem at a disadvantage compared to Arbor Realty's (ABR) other publicly traded preferreds. Series D (ABR.PR.D) and Series E (ABR.PR.E) are cheaper, yield more, and are no less senior in the capital stack than Series F.

However, ABR.PR.F's rate reset feature is unique and adds a safety layer against interest rate volatility that its sister preferreds don't have. Equally important, the reset date is approaching soon, and based on the coupon formula, ABR.PR.F could in fact turn from being the lowest dividend yielder to the highest among ABR's preferreds.

ABR's Challenges

ABR has been experiencing some portfolio challenges. The company provides bridge loans to property developers until a more permanent mortgage arrangement is secured, mostly to Government Sponsored Enterprises "GSEs" such as the Federal National Mortgage Association (FNMA), the Federal Home Loan Mortgage Association (FMCC), and other HUD programs. The company's investment portfolio stood at $12.1 billion as of December 2025.

GSEs work with institutional borrowers and property developers. They require that a property is stabilized - with minimum occupancy rates and recurring rent income - before they finance a property. ABR fills the timing gap between developing a property and stabilizing it. It does this by issuing bridge loans. As of December 2025, bridge loans represented 94% of ABR's portfolio (p.5).

An increasing number of ABR borrowers have been struggling to find permanent funding, most likely because of the inability to stabilize their properties. ABR has been modifying its bridge loans for its borrowers, lowering rates, deferring payments, extending maturities, and making other concessions to help its borrowers. Still, property ownership due to foreclosures has skyrocketed, roughly tripling in FY'25 compared to the prior year.

One of the ways we are resolving our legacy book is by resetting the interest rates on certain loans to today's market spreads, which puts these loans in a position to positively cover debt service from property operations without a shortfall - Ivan Kaufman, ABR's CEO, Q4'25 Earnings Call

Why I Am Bullish Now

The last time I covered ABR.PR.F was about 18 months ago. I argued that the preferreds, as a class, offer a better risk/reward opportunity, citing what I saw as early signs of a troubled portfolio.

My view is more constructive of ABR now, warranting a rating upgrade for ABR.PR.F from Hold to Buy. I believe that management has made significant progress in cleaning up its portfolio. Also, the scope of ABR's troubles has become clearer. The company took the necessary hits, including foreclosures and, in some cases, loan write-offs. But mostly the assets have proven to be of high quality.

Naturally, foreclosures and delinquencies tied up capital that would have otherwise been invested, and this has had a material impact on net income. Still, eventually, delinquent loans get a property foreclosure, and foreclosed properties get sold, untying capital, and bringing net income closer to average.

We estimate that these assets are creating a temporary drag of between $80 million to $100 million annually or roughly $0.40 to $0.48 a share, which translates into $0.10 to $0.12 a quarter of additional income that we are optimistic we will be able to create as we resolve the vast majority of these assets over the next several quarters - Paul Elenio, ABR's CFO, Q4'25 Earnings Call Transcript

For a holistic view of ABR, one needs to note its Agency Servicing business segment. This segment provides a more stable revenue stream. When a bridge loan is sold or committed to a GSE, ABR retains the right to service the loan. Servicing entails a variety of tasks, including loan collection and processing, record-keeping, and, in some cases, escrow payments when borrowers miss their payments. As of December 2026, Servicing revenue stood at $110 million, representing 40% of total revenue (p.40).

Series F Preferreds Yield and Price

As of the time of this writing, ABR.PR.F trades at $22.3/share, yielding about 7.0% annually on a 39-cent/share quarterly distribution. For comparison, Series D and Series E trade at $17.5/share and $17.4/share, yielding 9.1% and 9%, respectively. At first glance, Series D and E might appear more attractive, given their higher yield and lower price. However, Series F has an important feature, a rate reset that will transform its yield from fixed to variable. Starting October 2026, Series F yield will be determined based on the following formula: Coupon = (SOFR + 5.44%) times 25%. At the time of this writing, 3-month SOFR stands at 3.7%. This means that, starting October 2026, Series F's coupon will stand at $2.29 annually, distributed quarterly. Based on today's price of $22.3/share, this coupon payment translates to a yield of 10.28%, above Series D and Series F yields.

Series F also has a minimum rate floor feature set at 6.125%. When the rate becomes variable starting in October, the floor will prevent the coupon payment from decreasing below $1.53/share annually.

The Equity Buffer

ABR's accounting can be noisy. Management provides a non-GAAP profitability metric that's less volatile, which it calls Distributable Earnings, which it defines as GAAP net income adjusted for certain accounts. The largest adjustment in FY'25 was tied to Servicing Rights revenue, a revenue stream that could be lumpy, as it is recognized when a loan is packaged and sold to GSEs. The key point here is that Distributable Earnings appears to be a reliable profitability measure.

In FY'25, distributable earnings stood at $224 million, down from $358 million in FY'24, mirroring the increase in foreclosures and delinquencies as the company continues its efforts to manage its portfolio.

I believe that the company will cut its dividend this year. Distributable Earnings stood at $1.1/share, versus distributions, which now stand at $1.2/share.

Common equity holders always absorb the initial loss. For Series F investors, the equity buffer seems wide. Distributable earnings are net of interest and preferred dividend expense. The company is generating enough earnings to cover the preferred dividend ($41.4 million in FY'25) multiple times over.

Final Thoughts and Risks

Series F preferreds offer a unique risk/reward profile from Series D and Series E preferred shares. Although Series F yields lower rates compared to its sister preferreds, starting October 2026, this could change, assuming SOFR remains the same. If SOFR rises with inflation expectations, Series F could very well return yields much higher than the other series.

In my view, the main risk to this hypothesis is a deterioration in ABR's core business. The real estate market remains weak. The Federal Reserve Open Market Committee might be hesitant to cut rates, particularly given the inflationary geopolitical dynamics and international trade restrictions. It is worth noting that in May 2025, the president is set to nominate a new Federal Reserve chairman, who holds a single vote within the Open Market Committee.

Still, I believe that the equity buffer is comfortable for Series F preferred shares investors. Although the core business isn't flourishing, management seems to have made progress in containing distressed assets. For Series F investors, the key point is that ABR is still profitable on both GAAP, non-GAAP, and cash flow basis. The pre-preferred dividend coverage ratio stands at roughly 6.4x, based on distributable earnings of $224 million.

data.title: Sanmina: ZT Systems Doubles Revenue, Margins Hold, And The Selloff Is Unjustified
data.description:
Sanmina Corporation (SANM) is in a bit of a bind, in my opinion. It is beating its revenue and EPS estimates comfortably, and yet, the share price fell by 20% after it released Q1 FY26 results in late January. I believe that the dip represents a very attractive entry point, given that the price has fallen even further since then.

My position is based on the fact that the selloff is mostly a result of guidance optics, not any material change in the company’s numbers. For context, Sanmina is trading at ~$127 today, with multiples like 8x forward EBITDA and 12.6x forward P/E. It is a business that is fast becoming one of the largest US-based infrastructure manufacturers globally after buying ZT Systems from AMD last October, and it is trading at a major discount to every one of its comparable peers. The disconnect is why I’m initiating coverage with a Buy.

What Sanmina Is About

Sanmina is a Fortune 500 electronics manufacturing services company that co-founder Jure Sola has run as Chairman and CEO since 1980. The company operates across 20 countries and has roughly 39,000 employees today, and it builds complex electronic systems for OEMs in industrial, medical, defense, automotive, and cloud and AI infrastructure markets.

The business has two segments: Integrated Manufacturing Solutions (IMS), which handles the assembly side (circuit board assembly, system integration, logistics), and Components, Products, and Services (CPS), which is the more exciting segment, in my opinion. CPS is where the company’s higher-margin divisions across optical and microelectronics manufacturing, hyperscale storage platforms, defense, and aerospace work through SCI Technology and 42Q, a cloud-based manufacturing execution system running across more than 60 of Sanmina's own factories globally.

CPS is the more profitable segment of the two, and it brought in roughly 14.7% non-GAAP gross margins versus IMS at around 8.7% in the latest quarterly results (Q1 FY26), so I understand management's goal of growing the CPS mix. It’s the smart thing to do in terms of the financials.

As with most other globally relevant manufacturing and technology businesses, Sanmina’s USP is vertical integration. The company makes everything that goes into its own assembly lines. I’m talking about circuit boards, backplanes, cable assemblies, and machined components, among others, and that advantage means that it can afford to control its costs. Of course, the vertical integration also means that it is far more expensive for customers who have built programs around Sanmina's integrated supply chain to switch to another manufacturer.

I mentioned the ZT Systems acquisition in the intro, and I will discuss it in depth later, but I want to highlight some of the details here first. Sanmina bought the business from AMD in October 2025 for around $2.55 billion, and it now has the manufacturing capability for high-density AI data center infrastructure, including liquid cooling for GPU clusters that now run at rack densities the industry could not have imagined five years ago. It’s a blockbuster deal that basically changed what Sanmina’s identity will be over the next few years, and we can already see the results with some numbers. For example, cloud and AI infrastructure moved from 38% of Sanmina's revenue to 62% in just one quarter. That’s a completely different product mix, and I’m confident that it will continue to evolve in the near and medium term.

The Financial Picture: Before and After ZT

I don’t think it’s a stretch to say that FY2024 was a relatively down year for Sanmina. Its revenue fell 15.3% to $7.57 billion, mostly because customers across industrial, automotive, and communications end markets had to work through their excess inventory, so they weren’t ordering as much. That’s part of the cycle in the industry. For me, the way the company managed its cash flow and margins during that period is impressive. Management kept free cash flow at $231 million by cutting capex and working capital, and it kept operating margins roughly steady at 5.4% non-GAAP.

Conversely, FY2025 was rather nice, and I’d say that it was cleaner than the headline 7.4% revenue growth suggests. For example, the company expanded its non-GAAP operating margin by 30 basis points to 5.7%, and it increased its non-GAAP diluted EPS by 14.4% YoY to $6.04.

At the same time, it basically doubled free cash flow to $478 million, so we’re looking at a business that’s doing well with managing its working capital. It should go without saying, really, but a business generating $478 million in FCF on $8.1 billion in revenue, with no acquisition debt at that point, is in genuinely strong shape.

Anyway, all of those things were prior to the ZT deal. Below, I have compiled some of Sanmina’s numbers over the last 3 fiscal years, along with those from Q1 FY26.

Metric

FY2023

FY2024

FY2025

Q1 FY2026

Revenue

$8.94B

$7.57B

$8.13B

$3.19B

Non-GAAP Operating Margin

5.8%

5.4%

5.7%

6.0%

Non-GAAP Diluted EPS

$6.26

$5.28

$6.04

$2.38

Cash Flow from Operations

$235M

$340M

$621M

$179M

Free Cash Flow

$45M

$231M

$478M

$92M

Ending Cash

$668M

$626M

$926M

$1,415M

The first thing that’s immediately obvious from that table is the revenue figure in Q1 FY2026. The company made $3.19 billion in sales, up 59% year-over-year, and that was near the top of its guidance for the quarter. That number is nearly 40% of the total amount Sanmina made in FY25, so the scale is already showing through. The outlook for Q2 is between 3.1 and 3.4 billion, so at this rate, it will most definitely 1.5x its revenue scale in FY26. Of course, we’re still early in the fiscal year, but you can see why I’m bullish about the acquisition.

Besides that, the company recorded a non-GAAP operating margin of 6.0%, which is on par with its best pre-ZT quarterly performance. Non-GAAP diluted EPS of $2.38 beat the $2.14 consensus by 11%. AT the same time, Sanmina has ~$1.4 billion in cash against debts of roughly $2.17 billion, and a net leverage ratio of 0.8x. That’s a very manageable ratio for a business running at a $13-14 billion revenue run rate.

The Selloff Is An Overreaction

I believe that the post-earnings selloff happened primarily because of the guidance for Q2 FY26. The midpoint figure is $3.25 billion, and when we compare it to Q1’s $3.19 billion, it is essentially flat sequential growth. Some Wall Street firms and analysts have downgraded the stock because of it, and that has tanked its share price even more. That doesn’t make sense to me because the firm has beaten every single financial metric the market wants to see, and it just says that the market is ignoring the fact that management is still reiterating the $14 billion revenue target for the year.

Sure, only $6.3-6.4 billion of that will come in H1, but it is very reasonable to assume that the remaining $7.6 to $8 billion will materialize in H2. The company is telling us that the ramp is back-half weighted, and that makes sense because it aligns with how hyperscaler procurement cycles work. Besides, it is still working on the integrations and synergies from a new business, so we can expect some inefficiencies in the beginning.

I think the margin story also deserves some attention. One can make the argument that hyperscaler infrastructure assembly is lower-margin work that will drag the blended margin below the 5.7% non-GAAP operating margin that Sanmina earned in FY2025. That is a possibility because hyperscalers are typically cost-aggressive buyers, and rack assembly at volume is not the same margin profile as a high-complexity CPS component build. But as we’ve seen in Q1 FY2026, the company still delivered 6.0% non-GAAP operating margin on a revenue base dominated by ZT-class work.

I’d also like to point out a precedent for how this might play out. Celestica is another major player in the AI data center supply chain, and it entered earlier through its Hardware Platform Solutions business. Now, its adjusted operating margins are closer to 7.5%, a steady expansion from a few quarters ago, and this is a result of AI infrastructure scale growth. I can make the same argument for Sanmina because it can channel more of its own vertically manufactured components into ZT builds when the volumes ramp up. This means that the gross margins on ZT products will increase while the company spreads its fixed overhead costs across a larger revenue base. So, I think it’s reasonable to be confident in management’s plan to surpass 7% operating margins sooner rather than later.

Lastly, the macro backdrop makes it even harder to justify such a major selloff. For example, the International Data Corporation (IDC) recorded a 166% year-over-year growth in AI infrastructure spending in Q2 2025 alone. Then there’s McKinsey, whose research puts global data center capex through 2030 at $5.2 trillion. Last year alone, hyperscalers spent between $300 and $500 billion on AI infrastructure and data centers, depending on which reports you read, and they plan to spend even more this year.

Sanmina is one of the few companies with the scale, location (US base), and relationships to capture an even bigger share of that planned spend. Clearly, then, the tailwinds behind Sanmina’s business are much more solid than the market is giving it credit for.

The Numbers Make The Case

Sanmina’s share price today is around $127, its enterprise value is close to $8 billion, and its forward EV/EBITDA ratio is ~8x. The company’s EV/Sales multiple is ~0.6x, its non-GAAP P/E (FWD) ratio is 12.6x, and its P/S (FWD) ratio is ~0.5x.

Those metrics are 40-80% less than its sector medians, so that’s one obvious case for a re-rating. However, I’ll also compare Sanmina to peers like Celestica, Jabil, and Flex, so we have a clearer picture of how big the discount really is.

Sanmina has a similar level of exposure and revenue composition to Celestica in terms of AI infrastructure revenue concentration, and it has better growth and adjusted operating margin figures than both Jabil and Flex. Yet, we can see that it trades at a discount to all three on every multiple I mentioned above. Yes, it is working through an integration, so there’s a discount, but that doesn’t explain the gap between these companies. I certainly do not believe that it should be cheaper than a company like Flex, which has much smaller growth metrics and a lot less exposure to the AI tailwinds.

Now, if the market were to price the company’s forward EV/EBITDA at 10 or 11x, that would be on par with the latter two businesses in the table, but still with a 60-70% discount to Celestica. That gives us an implied equity value range of ~$169–$188/share.

I also ran a simple DCF model to gauge its value using some rather generous revenue growth and EPS calculations. I got a fair value that is in the same range as the multiples-based range, so clearly, the upside from today’s price cannot be overstated. As such, I’m attaching a 12-month price target of $185/share to the stock.

What Would Make Me Wrong

I’ve been rather bullish on Sanmina’s business so far, and now, I want to highlight some of the things that can break my thesis. The first and most serious threat is ZT integration failure. Yes, we’ve seen one clean quarter, but it doesn’t yet prove that the merger is done. A lot of the real work (things like consolidating manufacturing, holding hyperscaler relationships inherited from AMD, extracting vertical integration synergies, and delivering on the AMD NPI partnership) is multiyear execution, so there’s an extended timeframe we must consider. Besides, I also mentioned that hyperscaler procurement is ruthless. If a major cloud provider decides that Sanmina’s responsiveness has slipped or that it can find better terms with one of the company’s competitors, it’s not exactly a stretch to imagine that it will switch. Given that Sanmina now makes nearly two-thirds of its revenue from hyperscalers, I don’t think it can afford to lose a single customer because that would be a company‑scale event.

Then, there’s also the risk that AI infrastructure spending pauses. I think this is more likely than most people are willing to admit, but that’s beside the point here. We’ve seen hyperscalers throttle their capex before, in the 2022–2023 digestion cycle, and they’ll do it again if monetization lags capacity. Again, this ties back to Sanmina’s current revenue mix, and since it has less money coming from other sources, two soft quarters on the AI side would hit hard.

The third is margin dilution. Q1 held at 6.0%, and I’m confident that Q2 will be more of the same. But it is also possible that integration charges can affect the true run rate. Now, if the company’s blended margins settle closer to 5.5% because of hyperscaler pricing pressure, then we won’t have a clear path to Celestica‑like multiples. Right now, management’s >7% target requires real synergy capture and mix enrichment. It’s possible, but it’s not guaranteed.

Conclusion

Sanmina beat estimates across the board, held margins at the high end of its outlook on a business that has basically doubled in size, and still got sold down 20% because one guidance midpoint looked flat. I think that is an overreaction, and I think it is the kind of overreaction that creates real money for investors who don’t mind sitting through the noise. I’m going with a Buy rating, and I’m sure Q2 FY26 will provide the re-rating fuel that seems to be missing at this point.

data.title: China Gold Market Update: Resilient Demand In A Festive Month
data.description:
Gold’s diverging price trends from west to east

Gold prices diverged in February (Chart 1). The LBMA Gold Price PM in USD rose 4.8%, supported by factors such as heightened geopolitical risks and lower US Treasury yields, while the SHAUPM in RMB fell 1.3%, likely impacted by the 1.4% appreciation in RMB against the dollar and the Chinese New Year (CNY) holiday, which disrupted both local trading and physical gold withdrawals.

So far in March, gold prices have experienced mild declines as investor expectations of the Fed’s future rate path shift. But supported by rising safe-haven demand amid the Middle East chaos, the LBMA Gold Price PM remains above the US$5,000/oz threshold, while the SHAUPM stands firmly above RMB1,100/gram.

Chart 1: February saw gold prices take different directions

Monthly returns of the SHAUPM in RMB and LBMA Gold Price PM in USD*

Wholesale gold demand showed resilience

Gold withdrawals from the SGE totalled 85t in February, a CNY month, down 32% m/m (Chart 2), mainly due to fewer working days in February (14) compared to January (20).1 It is noteworthy that most factories in China – including jewellery manufacturers and bullion refiners – usually take longer CNY holidays than other sectors, further weighing on February activity.

Compared to February 2025, also a CNY month, wholesale gold demand fell by a modest 5t. This relative resilience was likely supported by two factors: first, a lower RMB gold price helped underpin some pre‑holiday restocking; and second, bullion sales remained robust, likely driven by seasonal factors and similar factors drove gold ETF demand detailed below.

Chart 2: Wholesale gold demand during CNY month stayed relatively resilient

Gold withdrawals from the SGE during CNY months*

Inflows into Chinese gold ETFs persisted

Chinese gold ETFs added RMB4.5bn (US$640mn) in February, marking the sixth consecutive monthly inflow (Chart 3). Collective holdings rose 3.6t to 290t in the month, another all-time high. But their total AUM fell 1% m/m to RMB331bn (US$48bn) as February inflows were insufficient to offset the local gold price drop.

Early‑month volatility may have prompted some investors to reduce their holdings. As the local gold price stabilised, investors added gold ETFs back into their portfolios. This is likely driven by both dip-buying as well as rising safe-haven demand amid geopolitical tensions globally and regionally. That said, fewer trading days due to the CNY holiday limited February inflows.

Inflows have accelerated so far in March, despite the gold price volatility. We believe rising safe-haven demand amid escalating global geopolitical tensions and a volatile equity market were main contributors.

Chart 3: Demand for Chinese gold ETFs continued in February

Chinese gold ETF demand and holdings in tonnes*

Chart 4: Trading volumes of gold futures rose in February

Daily average trading volumes of SHFE gold futures and the active gold futures price*

The PBoC reported another 1t addition to gold holdings in February, pushing the total to 2,309t (Chart 5). This now represents 10% of foreign exchange reserves, which rose 0.9% m/m to US$39tn. China’s gold reserves have risen consecutively for 16 months, sending an important message: in today’s world, gold’s role as an effective portfolio diversifier and uncertainty cushion is highly relevant.

Chart 5: Another addition to China’s gold reserves in February

The PBoC’s reported gold purchases and gold’s share of total foreign exchange reserves*

1The Chinese New Year holiday occurred between 15 and 23 February 2026.

Original Post

data.title: Adobe Q1 2026 Earnings Update
data.description:
At first glance, everything seems to be chugging along just fine at Adobe (ADBE). Their earnings on Thursday continued to show that revenue is still growing at a double-digit rate, with operating margins remaining quite stable.

In fact, Adobe mentioned their ARR from “AI first” offerings tripled YoY, with monthly active users (MAU) across Acrobat, Creative Cloud, Express and Firefly growing 17% YoY to reach 850 million. Moreover, creative freemium (which includes web and mobile versions of Firefly, Express, Premiere, Photoshop and Lightroom) MAU crossed 80 million, growing 50% YoY.

Given that context, Adobe shareholders may be forgiven if they wonder what exactly this company needs to do to get back in investors’ good books again. Let me suggest what they may need to accomplish to make investors rethink Adobe’s future.

Adobe shared the below datasheet in their press release on Thursday. I will particularly highlight the “Total Adobe ARR” row in this table. Notice that Adobe’s ARR (when adjusted for currency) has consistently decelerated for every single quarter in the last nine quarters.

In fact, it is the rate of deceleration that slightly accelerated in 1Q’26 which obviously doesn’t inspire a lot of confidence among investors. Adobe did mention that they experienced a “greater-than-anticipated decline” in their stock images book of business, which is a $450 million segment for them, but even if you completely adjusted for this segment, its ARR would grow at 11.2% YoY (FX adjusted) which still indicates deceleration.

If such deceleration continues, and given Adobe projects 10.2% ARR growth for FY’26, it is quite conceivable that the business may end up with HSD ARR growth by FY4Q’26.

As I have laid out in my “How Elephants May Die” piece, you don’t need to believe in a sudden decline in revenue for you to get concerned about a business’s long-term future, but a persistent deceleration can be sufficient to make you concerned.

Moreover, while Adobe’s margin doesn’t seem to show much of a pressure today, you will notice that Sales & Marketing (S&M) in the last four quarters has consistently grown at least ~200 bps faster than revenue. They managed to maintain their operating margins by keeping R&D expenses growth noticeably slower than revenue growth.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Milestone: Downgrading To Sell As CARDAMYST Faces A Tough Reality
data.description:
Overview

My last analysis of Milestone Pharmaceuticals (MIST) immediately followed the FDA approval of CARDAMYST (etripamil), a calcium channel blocker administered intranasally to terminate PSVT in adults. I spent a lot of time talking about the alternatives for “at-home” PSVT termination…like low-cost, generic, oral, and long-familiar antiarrhythmics like diltiazem. Although CARDAMYST, essentially, takes the obvious MoA and packages it in a branded nasal spray, I was uncertain if payers would dish out hundreds of dollars for the perceived convenience when an off-label oral costs a few single dollars.

I also discussed its pursuits in atrial fibrillation (Phase 3 ready), which is a much larger market but also has an existing, low-cost “pill-in-the-pocket” strategy.

I rated MIST a cautious “Hold,” warning that “there is a non-zero chance that CARDAMYST (...) may never see a return-on-investment (accounting for development and launch costs).”

Its stock has since fallen 27%.

Because the company has since launched CARDAMYST in the US, I wanted to take another look at MIST.

PSVT

CARDAMYST became available in late January with plans for a “national sales force to be deployed in mid-February 2026.”

Now, its WAC is ~$1649 per prescription, but management expects net prices between $500 and $1000.

We've chosen this price in part because of the value prop that CARDAMYST represents relative to the other treatment modalities, which insurance companies must reimburse, whether it's emergency department visits or hospitalizations or ablations.

Management leans heavily on CARDAMYST’s potential to decrease ER visits or hospitalizations, but there is no such data that confirms CARDAMYST’s efficacy here. In an article published in The Lancet, while there was a numerical trend in ER visits favoring CARDAMYST, it did not conclusively beat out a placebo.

There were lower percentages of patients seeking additional medical interventions (e.g., intravenous adenosine) and emergency department visits in the etripamil group than in the placebo group, but significance was not shown.

In fact, if you view the “Supplementary appendix” and scroll down to Table S7, you will see that CARDAMYST did not come close to stat sig (p=0.209; 14% ER visits for etripamil vs. 21% for placebo).

So, in my view, CARDAMYST has not proven a concrete, payer-critical advantage (e.g., ER visits, ablations, etc.) over oral generics. This could make it challenging to gain market share.

You could say “convenience” is CARDAMYST’s differentiating factor, but this takes a hit when you consider that 65% of CARDAMYST patients used a second dose in its pivotal studies…implying a two-step rescue regimen for most patients.

I do believe most of my inputs are actually optimistic, and I’m going to explain why.

Estimates vary, but there are likely ~2M US patients with PSVT. The majority rely on daily preventative medications. I estimated that 600k will be on some kind of pill-in-the-pocket strategy. This is CARDAMYST’s actual TAM. My estimates are largely in-line with management’s.

$750 net price is management’s midpoint estimate, which I find aggressive given what I pointed out above.

A 10% peak share assumes there are 60,000 (+1% per annum) US patients on CARDAMYST at its peak, which is a lot of people. This assumes that many of the “10,000” prescribers that Milestone is targeting will be very aware of CARDAMYST and use it often in practice.

Some will argue that CARDAMYST patients will require multiple doses per year. While I acknowledge this, I believe my peak share estimate of 10% and management-friendly net price assumption of $750 provide a hedge against this uncertainty (prescriptions per year).

My model also effectively assumes that Milestone will be immediately cash-generative (60% launch opex, 10% COGS), which very likely understates initial launch/marketing opex.

I end up modeling CARDAMYST in PSVT for roughly $50M in peak annual sales…worth $107.4M in NPV.

AFib-RVR

Interestingly, Milestone’s Phase 3 trial (ReVeRA-301) in AFib-RVR, while operationally “ready to start,” is still in purgatory. It’s listed on clinicaltrials.gov, but is “not yet recruiting,” with an estimated study start of “2027-01.”

Mind you, Phase 2 data was revealed in late 2023.

It’s totally possible that management is being very cautious with its spending ahead of the PSVT launch, which is not unreasonable. That said, it does materially impact Launch Year and, subsequently, Peak Year and rNPV.

My modeling assumptions for AFib-RVR are not all too different from PSVT.

Because AFib-RVR is a much larger TAM and has a standardized pill-in-the-pocket strategy already in place (PSVT is generally less standardized), my peak share assumption is lower (5%). Of course, because CARDAMYST is merely in Phase 3 for this indication, the Probability of Success was set to 65%...which is higher than the average Phase 3 POS (given that calcium channel blockers are known to work in AFib-RVR).

Just in case I’m being too cautious with that 65% estimate, the image above does show how changes to POS assumptions impact rNPV in the Sensitivity Analysis.

SOTP

Here’s how everything looks:

MIST's fair value per share comes back at $1.38, which is ~18% under its current price, implying modest overvaluation. Because I did not model for any dilution, the fair value per share is just the intrinsic equity value of $188.7M ($107.4M rNPV for CARDAMYST in PSVT + $25.4M rNPV for CARDAMYST in AFib-RVR + $26.6M for ROW pipeline value + $123M pro forma cash − $36.7M in projected corporate overhead until profitability is reached − $57M pro forma debt) divided by MIST's pro forma shares outstanding of 136.6M.

Because I feel that many of my assumptions (discussed earlier in the article) leaned optimistic, MIST being flagged as 17.8% "overvalued" per my model concerns me.

Scenario Analysis

My Scenario Analysis combines my "Base" case estimates with “Bull” and “Bear” scenarios in an effort to be objective.

The “Bear” case reflects what I alluded to in my last MIST analysis: it is totally possible that CARDAMYST never achieves ROI. In this event, MIST could be valued at ~$0.00/share. “Bear” case assumptions do not strike me as silly. Assumptions like $600 net price, 12% WACC, and 2.7% peak share (combined) in these large indications do not seem unreasonably bearish to me.

The "Bull" case assumes 88.8% POS, which is very high given that the AFib-RVR indication is merely Phase 3 ready. Peak share is set at 10.1% and net price at $1k, which is the upper-end of management's net price estimate (between $500 and $1k). "Bull" case assumptions appear overzealous to me.

The probability-adjusted weight (50% Base/25% Bull/25% Bear) comes back at $1.51. While higher than my "Base" case estimate of $1.38/share, it still falls ~10% short of MIST's current price.

Financial Health

Because Milestone does not report FY25 results until next week (3/20/2026), I’m going off of Q3 data here.

At the end of Q3 (Sept. 30), Milestone had roughly $83M in cash and investments. It owes $56.206M in “senior secured convertible notes” due 2029. Milestone has a Royalty Purchase Agreement on sales of CARDAMYST. They raised an additional $75M from this. The royalties are tiered. My model reflects 5% (“Ownership Split”).

Now, pro forma estimates on cash and shares are a bit complicated because there are many moving parts, but I did my best to summarize below:

Again, these are just my best estimates. These values should be verified/updated when management reports on March 20th.

Because management did not provide its own cash runway estimate in its Q3 PR, I will do so myself.

Strictly using historical values, Milestone’s “cash from operations” (a common proxy for “cash burn”) was $43.1M in the trailing twelve months (or roughly $11M/quarter). If we divide this figure by its pro forma cash estimate of $123M, it gives Milestone just under three years of cash runway.

However, Milestone’s cash burn is likely to go up as it launches CARDAMYST in PSVT and begins testing it in a Phase 3 AFib-RVR trial. So, the actual cash runway could be materially shorter than three years.

Although I modeled Milestone as “fully funded,” I think that is an optimistic assumption. Therefore, investors should be aware of the potential for more dilution if management needs more cash.

Conclusion

Modeling CARDAMYST in PSVT and AFib-RVR has put things into perspective for me. I think MIST merits more caution than just a “Hold” rating (downgrade to “Sell”).

In my view, Milestone does not have the data to achieve broad market adoption over generics for CARDAMYST. Despite the large target populations, compressed net pricing and limited peak share assumptions really put a damper on CARDAMYST’s potential. And even though I may have understated Milestone’s runway to profitability, my model still has MIST as overvalued, with a real potential for, essentially, unlimited downside in a, what appears to be, realistic “Bear” scenario.

Some things that could change my mind include a ROW partnership/launch that proves my 0.2x ROW multiple to be far too conservative, overwhelmingly positive Phase 3 AFib-RVR data that shows stat sig improvements on payer-critical endpoints like ER visit reduction, real-world evidence that supports CARDAMYST's value in PSVT, and stronger-than-expected market uptake in PSVT with better-than-expected GTN.

Risks

Because MIST is a microcap stock, I also want to take a moment to discuss some microcap-specific risks.

As I alluded to earlier, there is potential for substantial dilution, especially as its market capitalization is ~$150M. For example, raising just $50M in equity would result in ~33% dilution.

Other common microcap risks include volatility, low liquidity, lack of coverage, and high risk of total loss.

data.title: Risks Rise For Insurers As War Pressures On Iran Mount
data.description:
Insurers providing war cover in Persian Gulf countries face higher risks as the US and Israel continue their air campaign against Iran.

Iran has hit a range of non-military targets in the Gulf in retaliation for the US-Israeli airstrikes, which began Feb. 28.

Attacks on critical infrastructure in Gulf states are likely to continue as the US and Israel degrade Iran's longer-range attack capabilities, target critical Iranian infrastructure and the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) operates more autonomously, according to Jack Kennedy, head of Middle East and North Africa country risk at S&P Global Market Intelligence.

"With Iran's command chain progressively targeted, military units appear to be shifting to a doctrine of decentralized operations, increasing the likelihood of opportunistic strikes," Kennedy said in emailed comments March 9.

Range of risks

While Iran has targeted US military infrastructure in Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) countries, it has also hit civilian property, including hotels, airports and energy facilities. Hotels in Dubai and Bahrain were attacked early in the conflict, while Abu Dhabi National Oil Co. was forced shut a refinery after suffering a fire caused by drone strikes. Bahrain's energy company, Bapco Energies B.S.C. Closed, declared force majeure on its group operations following an attack on its refinery complex.

A possible early example of the IRGC's autonomy was the attack on the Port of Duqm in Oman, for which the Iranian regime reportedly apologized, according to Bilal Bassiouni, head of risk forecasting at risk advisory firm Pangea-Risk.

"We may see more similar situations where the IRGC or military leadership take on ad-hoc responsibilities [for choosing targets]," Bassiouni said in a March 3 interview. That heightens the risk for a wider variety of assets. The likelihood of Iranian strikes on energy facilities grows the more the US and Israeli air campaign intensifies and the more cornered the regime feels, Bassiouni said.

Property could also be damaged in attacks on Israel in response to its assaults on Iran itself and Iran-backed armed group Hezbollah. Israel has been hitting Hezbollah positions in Lebanon along with its strikes on Iran.

Property damaged in attacks would trigger claims under political violence and war-on-land policies for insurers and reinsurers. Moody's Ratings in a March 11 report said political violence and terrorism underwriters would start receiving loss reports "in the coming days." One of the first large claims notifications is likely to be from the attacks on refineries belonging to Bapco Energy, Moody's said.

The risks to property on land come on top of the threats to shipping in the Gulf, particularly vessels that try to pass through the Strait of Hormuz. The risks to shipping in the region have caused war insurance prices to surge. The UK Maritime Trade Operations Centre (UKMTO), which provides shipping security information, received 16 reports of attacks on vessels in and around the Persian Gulf, Gulf of Oman and the Strait of Hormuz between Feb. 28 and March 12.

Aviation claims are not out of the question either.

"Underwriters are paying closer attention to ground accumulations, particularly at major hubs, such as Dubai, and are asking more detailed questions around routing, airport selection and contingency planning for aircraft relocation," Phil Smaje, global industry specialty leader for transportation and logistics at insurance broker Aon plc said in emailed comments.

Cyber and trade

Cyberattacks are also a possible risk for insurers. Medical equipment manufacturer Stryker said March 11 that it is suffering from a cyberattack affecting its internal Microsoft environment. Handala, a hacking group linked to Iran, has claimed responsibility for the attack and said it was a response to the bombing of the Minab school in Iran, the Guardian reported.

There have been no public reports so far of large insured cyber losses directly attributable to the Middle East war, S&P Global Ratings said in a March 11 report. Larger, state-linked cyberattacks could "severely test underwriting models" and lead to legal disputes over coverage, the report said.

If the war puts significant pressure on the global economy, insurers and reinsurers could face claims in lines such as trade credit insurance, which covers the risk of suppliers not getting paid for goods or services. Credit insurers are closely monitoring the implications of the war for global supply chains and trade flows, particularly if disruption becomes more prolonged, Oliver Henderson, chief broking officer for credit solutions at Aon, said in comments emailed to the press.

"What matters most from an underwriting perspective is how delays and interruptions translate into liquidity pressure over time," Henderson said. Underwriting appetite is still "broadly intact" with insurers taking a "more selective, scenario-based approach."

Should the conflict drag on and trigger supply chain disruptions and inflation, even insurers writing standard covers like personal motor in countries far beyond the war zone could face rising claims bills.

Claims uncertainty

Potential claims levels from the war remain highly uncertain. Insurance and reinsurance industry executives have been reluctant to make any estimates, though they expect some losses.

There is a chance that damage could be under-reported, Bassiouni said, as Gulf states seek to protect their reputations.

"This is a doomsday scenario for a lot of Gulf countries," he said, as some of their economic models are based on being secure and free from violence. "The views of drone strikes and ballistic missiles around skyscraper buildings in the Gulf is quite detrimental to that image."

The marine business has seen higher claims notifications, according to Jonathan Moss, managing partner of law firm DWF's London office. However, only a percentage of these will translate into actual claims. Coverage disputes between policyholders and marine insurers and their reinsurers are also likely, Moss said in an interview March 4.

If the war is short-lived, insurers should be able to cope with the claims bills, as most property and casualty policies exclude war as standard.

Cloudy endgame

Moody's said in its March 11 report that its baseline scenario is that the conflict will last a matter of weeks and that passage of ships through the Strait of Hormuz will resume "at scale."

US President Donald Trump said March 2 that the war was projected to last four to five weeks but could go on for longer. Trump then told CBS News in a March 9 interview that the war is "very complete, pretty much."

"De-escalation is unlikely in the near term," Market Intelligence's Kennedy said. "The US and Israeli campaign is expected to last weeks or longer unless pressured by financial markets, energy prices, or domestic political factors. Iran's leadership has rejected negotiations, and the IRGC is expanding its attacks."

A wind-down would likely come from the US stopping its campaign after achieving specific military targets, Kennedy said. Iran might reduce its retaliation if attacks it alleges are being launched from Gulf Cooperation Council states stop, but the IRGC becoming de-centralized "makes consistent de-escalation difficult."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Backtests, Causality, And Model Risk In Quantitative Investing
data.description:
By Igor Oliveira

Quantitative finance continues to debate the reliability and limits of model-driven investment strategies. One central question is how much weight investors should place on backtesting.

In The Factor Mirage: How Quant Models Go Wrong, Marcos López de Prado, PhD, and Vincent Zoonekynd, PhD, outline why investors should move beyond accepting historical performance at face value and focus on understanding why a model works. That is a valuable contribution to strengthening the rigor of quantitative investing — and one that invites further reflection on how that reasoning is structured.

It may help to frame the issue not as a binary choice between correlation and causation, but as a layered problem in which different forms of reasoning play distinct roles.

In practice, the choice is rarely between simple correlation and fully specified causality. Most investment research operates somewhere in between. Sometimes we can describe and test a mechanism directly. Sometimes we cannot. The system may move too quickly, key variables may be only partially observable, or the time and resources required to build a richer model may not be available.

In those settings, association-based reasoning still has value. That is not a defect of finance; it is a general feature of decision-making under uncertainty.

Association Under Constraint

Human beings often rely on associations when there is no time to construct a full causal account. That is not necessarily irrational; it can be adaptive. A fast association can guide action before slower, more elaborate reasoning is possible.

The same is true in investment practice. When relevant drivers cannot be directly observed or causal structure is only partly understood, associational signals may still contain useful information.
